skill.name: buffer-calculator
skill.description: Calculate precise buffer recipes with accurate mass and volume measurements for molecular biology and biochemistry. Supports PBS, RIPA, and TAE with concentration scaling, stock solution preparation, pH adjustment guidance, and step-by-step protocols.
skill.summary: Precise buffer recipe calculations for molecular biology and biochemistry experiments.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 50 static analysis findings are false positives. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' alerts match non-cryptographic terms in an audit results JSON file and standard library references. 'Certificate/key files' alerts match Python dictionary .keys() method calls, not actual certificate files. 'System reconnaissance' alerts match standard CLI output (print statements) and descriptive text. 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' alerts match markdown code block delimiters used for documentation examples. 'Hardcoded URL' alerts match legitimate scientific reference citations to Cold Spring Harbor Protocols and Thermo Fisher. The critical heuristic combination finding (code execution + network + credential access) is incorrect - this skill performs only local calculations with no network access, no credential access, and no code injection vectors. All detected patterns are safe documentation and citation content.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm (False Positive - All Instances)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' at 14 locations across buffer-calculator_audit_result_v2.json, scripts/main.py, and SKILL.md. All locations contain non-cryptographic content: the audit JSON file contains terms like 'PASS' and 'result' that triggered a false match; scripts/main.py:111 is argparse.ArgumentParser initialization; SKILL.md lines 3, 26-27, 115, 139 are documentation headers and error handling text. No cryptographic operations exist anywhere in the skill.

low_findings.title: Certificate/Key Files (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'Certificate/key files' at scripts/main.py:126 and SKILL.md:58. Both locations contain Python .keys() method calls on a dictionary (calc.BUFFER_RECIPES.keys()) used to list available buffer types. These are standard Python dictionary key access operations, not TLS certificate files or cryptographic key material.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'System reconnaissance' at 5 locations. In scripts/main.py:103-104 these are print() statements for standard buffer preparation instructions. In SKILL.md:16 and 46 these are feature descriptions and error messages for buffer type validation. In the audit JSON file lines 77 and 214 these are descriptive evaluation text. None of these involve any system discovery or information gathering behavior.

low_findings.title: Ruby/Shell Backtick Execution (False Positive - All Instances)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 20 locations for 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' in SKILL.md. All instances are markdown fenced code blocks (triple backticks ```) used for documentation examples. SKILL.md contains Python code blocks showing how to use the BufferCalculator class and bash examples showing CLI usage. The markdown formatting characters are not shell execution operators. No actual backtick command execution exists.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL (False Positive - All Instances)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 4 URL locations in SKILL.md. Line 7 contains a GitHub source attribution URL pointing to the aipoch/medical-research-skills repository. Lines 181-182 contain reference citations to Cold Spring Harbor Protocols (cshprotocols.org) and Thermo Fisher Scientific buffers page. All URLs are legitimate scientific reference sources commonly cited in laboratory protocols. No URL performs data exfiltration or connects to unauthorized services.

low_findings.title: Critical Heuristic: Dangerous Combination (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static scanner reported a critical heuristic finding for 'Code execution + Network + Credential access' combination. Evaluation confirms this is incorrect. The skill is a pure calculation tool with: (a) no code execution beyond the documented python scripts/main.py invocation, (b) no network calls whatsoever (URLs are documentation citations only), (c) no credential or environment variable access. The script reads no environment variables, makes no HTTP requests, and performs only deterministic local calculations.

content.user_title: Calculate Laboratory Buffer Recipes with Precision
content.value_statement: Molecular biology and biochemistry researchers spend significant time calculating buffer formulations with correct molecular weights. This skill instantly computes precise mass and volume measurements for PBS, RIPA, TAE, and other common buffers with step-by-step protocols.
actual_capabilities: Calculate precise component masses for PBS, RIPA, and TAE buffers with correct molecular weights
Scale recipes from 1X working concentration to 10X, 20X, or higher stock solutions
Handle both solid component masses (milligrams) and liquid volume measurements (milliliters)
Provide step-by-step preparation protocols with safety notes and pH adjustment guidance
Calculate custom buffer recipes using the documented mass formula with user-provided inputs
Support multiple buffer types including HEPES, Tris-HCl, and MOPS for various applications
limitations: Does not verify pH after preparation - requires separate pH meter verification
Only supports buffer types in the predefined library or custom recipes with user-provided molecular weights
Cannot automatically account for hydrate variations in molecular weights
Does not validate chemical compatibility or long-term stability of custom formulations

use_cases.title: Prepare PBS for Cell Washing
use_cases.description: A lab technician needs 500 mL of 1X PBS for immunofluorescence staining. The skill calculates exact masses of NaCl, KCl, and phosphates needed with the correct formula.

use_cases.title: Create Concentrated Stock Solutions
use_cases.description: A researcher preparing weekly experiments calculates 10X TAE stock solution to save time on buffer preparation for DNA electrophoresis across multiple runs.

use_cases.title: Scale Recipes for Small Volumes
use_cases.description: A graduate student needs 50 mL of RIPA buffer for a small-scale protein extraction. The skill scales down the standard formulation to match the reduced volume.

prompt_templates.title: Calculate a Standard Buffer Recipe
prompt_templates.prompt: Calculate a 1X PBS buffer recipe for 500 mL final volume. Include component masses, preparation steps, and pH information.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user needs a standard lab buffer

prompt_templates.title: Create a Stock Solution
prompt_templates.prompt: Calculate a 10X TAE stock solution for 1000 mL. Provide dilution instructions to make 1X working solution.
prompt_templates.scenario: Researcher preparing bulk stock for daily use

prompt_templates.title: Custom Buffer Formulation
prompt_templates.prompt: Calculate a custom buffer with 50 mM Tris, 150 mM NaCl, and 1 mM EDTA at pH 8.0 for 250 mL total volume. Use molecular weights: Tris 121.14, NaCl 58.44, EDTA 372.24.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs a buffer not in the standard library

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot Buffer pH Issues
prompt_templates.prompt: I prepared a 1X PBS buffer but the pH measures 6.8 instead of 7.4. Run the PBS calculation for 500 mL and explain common causes for pH deviation.
prompt_templates.scenario: User troubleshooting a failed buffer preparation

output: 1. Dissolve all solid components in ~400 mL distilled water
2. Adjust pH to 7.4 using HCl or NaOH if needed
3. Bring to final volume of 500 mL with distilled water
Dilution: Mix 100 mL 10X stock + 900 mL distilled water for 1X TAE
Instructions: Dissolve Tris and EDTA in water, add acetic acid, adjust pH to 8.0
Note: SDS and Triton X-100 are detergents. Wear gloves during preparation.
Add protease inhibitors fresh before use for protein extraction protocols.

best_practices: Always verify molecular weights for hydrated compounds before calculating to prevent errors from anhydrous versus hydrated form confusion.
Use a calibrated pH meter to verify buffer pH after preparation instead of relying on calculated values.
Document the concentration multiplier assumption explicitly in all calculations to prevent 10X versus 1X confusion.
anti_patterns: Requesting drug formulation or chemical synthesis tasks outside the buffer library scope will not work and wastes time.
Assuming buffer pH is correct without meter verification can compromise sensitive experiments like enzyme assays.
Using incorrect molecular weights by confusing anhydrous and hydrated compound forms leads to inaccurate concentrations.

faq.question: What buffer types does this calculator support?
faq.answer: The calculator supports PBS, RIPA, TAE, HEPES, Tris-HCl (pH 7.4 and 8.0), and MOPS. You can also request custom buffer calculations with your own component list and molecular weights.

faq.question: How does the mass calculation formula work?
faq.answer: The formula is: mass (mg) = concentration (mM) x volume (mL) x molecular weight (g/mol) / 1000. For percentage-based components like detergents, a volume percentage calculation is used instead.

faq.question: Can I create custom buffers not in the library?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide the component names, their target concentrations in mM, and molecular weights in g/mol. The calculator will apply the standard formula to compute required masses.

faq.question: Does this skill handle pH adjustment automatically?
faq.answer: The skill provides target pH values and guidance for pH adjustment, but it does not calculate the exact amount of acid or base needed. You must use a pH meter after preparation.

faq.question: Can I scale recipes from stock solutions?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the concentration multiplier parameter (e.g., 10X, 20X) to scale recipes. The output includes dilution instructions for working concentration preparation.

faq.question: What precision can I expect from the calculations?
faq.answer: Solid component masses are calculated to milligram precision (2 decimal places) and liquid components to 2 decimal places in milliliters. Actual lab scale precision depends on your equipment.
